From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Palanquin Shrine in Shinto

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg/250px-Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg)

A *mikoshi* of [Hiyoshi-taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi-taisha "Hiyoshi-taisha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Nada_no_Kenka_matsuri_%2C_himeji01.JPG/250px-Nada_no_Kenka_matsuri_%2C_himeji01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nada_no_Kenka_matsuri_,_himeji01.JPG)

*Mikoshi* fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [Himeji](/wiki/Himeji "Himeji")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg/250px-ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg)](/wiki/File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg)

This *mikoshi* enshrines [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") at the [Tōshō-gū](/wiki/T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Tōshō-gū") in [Nikkō](/wiki/Nikk%C5%8D "Nikkō").

A ****mikoshi**** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 神輿) is a sacred religious [palanquin](/wiki/Palanquin "Palanquin") (also translated as portable [Shinto shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine")). [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the *mikoshi* resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [veranda](/wiki/Veranda "Veranda") and a railing.

Often the [Japanese honorific](/wiki/Japanese_honorific "Japanese honorific") prefix **o-** (お) is added, making ****omikoshi**** (お神輿).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mikoshi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first recorded use of *mikoshi* was during the [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period"). Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [Hachiman](/wiki/Hachiman "Hachiman") is said to have been carried from [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu") to [Nara](/wiki/Nara_Prefecture "Nara Prefecture") to worship the newly-constructed *[Daibutsu](/wiki/Daibutsu "Daibutsu")* at [Tōdai-ji](/wiki/T%C5%8Ddai-ji "Tōdai-ji").[[1]](#cite_note-usa-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) As the head shrine of all [Hachiman shrines](/wiki/Hachiman_shrine "Hachiman shrine") in Japan, [Usa Jingū](/wiki/Usa_Jing%C5%AB "Usa Jingū") in [Ōita Prefecture](/wiki/%C5%8Cita_Prefecture "Ōita Prefecture"), Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of *mikoshi*.[[1]](#cite_note-usa-1)

## Shapes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mikoshi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Shapes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi_2018.jpg/250px-JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi_2018.jpg)

A *mikoshi* in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/%E3%82%AE%E3%83%A3%E3%83%AB%E7%A5%9E%E8%BC%BF%EF%BC%92.JPG/250px-%E3%82%AE%E3%83%A3%E3%83%AB%E7%A5%9E%E8%BC%BF%EF%BC%92.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E3%82%AE%E3%83%A3%E3%83%AB%E7%A5%9E%E8%BC%BF%EF%BC%92.JPG)

Woman *mikoshi*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Children_carry_mikoshi_Sanja_Matsuri_2006.JPG/250px-Children_carry_mikoshi_Sanja_Matsuri_2006.JPG)](/wiki/File:Children_carry_mikoshi_Sanja_Matsuri_2006.JPG)

Child *mikoshi* ([Sanja Matsuri](/wiki/Sanja_Matsuri "Sanja Matsuri"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg/250px-Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg)

Japan's largest ([Tomioka Hachiman Shrine](/wiki/Tomioka_Hachiman_Shrine "Tomioka Hachiman Shrine"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Edo_meisho_d%C5%8Dke_zukushi%2C_T%C5%8Dri-itch%C5%8Dme_Gion-e_by_HiroKage.jpg/250px-Edo_meisho_d%C5%8Dke_zukushi%2C_T%C5%8Dri-itch%C5%8Dme_Gion-e_by_HiroKage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edo_meisho_d%C5%8Dke_zukushi,_T%C5%8Dri-itch%C5%8Dme_Gion-e_by_HiroKage.jpg)

[Utagawa Hirokage](/wiki/Utagawa_Hirokage "Utagawa Hirokage")

Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(mythology) "Phoenix (mythology)").

## Festival and flow

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mikoshi&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Festival and flow")]

During a *[matsuri](/wiki/Matsuri "Matsuri")* (Japanese festival) involving a *mikoshi*, people bear the *mikoshi* on their shoulders by means of two, four, or (rarely) six poles. They bring the *mikoshi* from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called *uma* (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the *mikoshi* in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called *o-hamaori*). At some festivals, the people who bear the *mikoshi* wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")) inside.

## Methods of shouldering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mikoshi&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Methods of shouldering")]

The most common method of shouldering in Japan is *hira-katsugi* (平担ぎ) "flat carry". Bearers chant *wasshoi* (わっしょい) and may or may not toss and shake the *mikoshi*.

Other methods include:

(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.

* *Edomae* (江戸前) "[Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo") style" is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [Asakusa Sanja Festival](/wiki/Sanja_Matsuri "Sanja Matsuri"). The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya ... etc". The *mikoshi* is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left.
* "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [Shonan](/wiki/Shonan "Shonan") in [Kanagawa](/wiki/Kanagawa "Kanagawa") Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The *mikoshi* is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song".
* Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [Odawara](/wiki/Odawara "Odawara") (next to the [Hakone](/wiki/Hakone "Hakone")). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple *mikoshi* meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah" and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The bearers do not sway the mikoshi.
* In this "united" style, the *mikoshi* uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
